HCDE 525 — Emerging Issues Presentation

Virtual Reality & the UX Gap

Introduction — Slides 1–3  ·  Angel Diaz
01 — Introduction

A concept in search
of a human

History & Context — the throughline

1968
The First Window Into a Virtual World
The Sword of Damocles — Ivan Sutherland, MIT
Ivan Sutherland's head-mounted display was the first device to place a human inside a computer-generated environment. So heavy it required a ceiling mount, it proved the concept was possible long before the technology could make it comfortable.
1984
VR Gets Its Name — and Its Vision
Jaron Lanier coins "Virtual Reality" — VPL Research
Jaron Lanier coined the term "virtual reality" and founded VPL Research, the first company to sell commercial VR systems including haptic gloves and shared virtual environments. He established VR not as a simulation tool but as a medium for human expression and collaborative experience.
1995
VR Meets the Mass Market — and Loses
The first consumer crash — Nintendo VirtualBoy
The VirtualBoy was Nintendo's attempt to bring VR to a mass consumer audience — the first time the technology landed in homes at scale. It was discontinued within a year, selling fewer than 800,000 units worldwide due to widespread reports of headaches, eye strain, and nausea.
2012
The Modern VR Era Begins
Oculus Rift Kickstarter — $2.4M raised
Palmer Luckey's Oculus Rift Kickstarter raised $2.4M in 2012, reigniting mainstream interest in VR after nearly two decades of dormancy. It triggered a new wave of investment and development that led directly to the modern headset landscape — and prompted Facebook's $2B acquisition of Oculus in 2014.
Now
The Virtual Touches the Physical
VR meets the physical world — Princeton, 2025
Princeton researchers demonstrated mixed reality headsets paired with physical robots, enabling gesture-based control of real objects from inside a virtual environment. For the first time, actions taken in virtual space produce direct consequences in the physical world.
02 — Introduction

Reality is a spectrum

Extended Reality — from digital overlay to full immersion

XR — Extended Reality

The umbrella term for all technologies that blend physical and digital worlds

physical
virtual
AR — Augmented Reality
Digital overlay
in physical space
You see the world. Information appears on top.
No interaction between digital and physical elements. The real environment is fully visible — digital content floats over it. eg. Google Maps AR, AccuVein, heads-up displays.
MR — Mixed Reality
Digital objects,
physical space
Digital objects respond to your real environment.
Physical and digital elements interact. A holographic object casts a shadow on your table, responds when you touch it. eg. Apple Vision Pro, HoloLens, robot teleoperation.
VR — Virtual Reality
Full immersion
into the virtual space
The physical world is completely blocked out.
You exist entirely in a digital environment. Every sensory input comes from the virtual world. eg. Meta Quest, surgical simulation, exposure therapy.
OR orientation training — full VR simulation, first-person surgical perspective
Exposure therapy — clinician-guided PTSD & anxiety treatment in VR
03 — Case Study

Why VR needs UX

A $70B case study in designing without the human

Case study — Horizon Worlds, 2021–2024
$70 billion invested. 38 daily users.
$70B+
Meta Reality Labs investment
2021–2024
38
Decentraland active daily users
at peak, 2022
1,500+
Reality Labs jobs cut as the
metaverse pivot collapsed, 2026
What the industry prioritized
01 Scale & infrastructure
02 Platform dominance
03 Technical novelty
What humans wanted
01 Authentic connection
02 Comfort first
03 Human presence
Evidence — from inside Meta
"For an experience to become delightful and retentive, it must first be usable and well crafted."
— Vishal Shah, VP of Metaverse, Meta — internal memo, 2022
"User experience, navigation, and visuals fell short of expectations. There is a mismatch between Meta's focus on scalability and people's desire for high-quality, immersive experiences."
— Alex Kim, CMO, Sensorium, 2022
The industry thought it was
A technical
problem
The actual question was
How do humans
want to connect?
04 — Analysis

Three challenges. One discipline.

Where the VR industry falls short — and where UX steps in

01 — Cybersickness
Meta Quest / Beat Saber
The body rejects what the eyes believe.
22–80%
of VR users experience cybersickness
↗ Nature, 2021
Opportunity
Prioritize comfort-first locomotion — frame rate and movement method are design decisions, not engineering ones.
Solution in practice
Teleportation-based movement reduces nausea by up to 80% vs. joystick locomotion. Frame rate matters: 90fps → 60fps increases nausea ~40%.
02 — The Isolation Paradox
Social VR — VRChat, Horizon Worlds
Built to connect people. Physically isolates them.
1 in 3
adults felt lonely at least once a week in 2025 — yet VR platforms marketed as social spaces remain largely empty
↗ Frontiers in VR, 2021
Opportunity
Design for co-located awareness — keep the physical room present.
Proposed direction
Passthrough modes and spectator screens let VR coexist with the physical room — isolation is a design choice, not a requirement.
03 — Weight & Ergonomics
Apple Vision Pro
The body gives out before the session ends.
600g+
At 600g — nearly twice the weight of AirPods Max (380g) — the Vision Pro exceeds what most users find comfortable for extended wear. Neck strain was reported within 30–60 min, even by internal Apple testers.
↗ Tom's Guide
Opportunity
Distribute weight, don't just reduce it — design for full-session comfort.
Solution in practice
Balance over lightness — rear battery placement and dual-loop headbands shift load off the front. The goal is wearability across a full session.
05 — Outlook

Where VR is going

Four trajectories — and the UX problem space each one opens

Spatial Computing
What's happening
VR and the physical world are converging
Watch for
Confusion at the AR/physical boundary — are users unclear about what's real, what's digital, and what the rules are in that space?
Traditional UI research methods may not apply — how does our approach to designing and studying interfaces change when the environment is the interface?
AI + VR
What's happening
Environments that respond, adapt, and generate themselves
Watch for
AI-driven unpredictability — can users anticipate how the AI will behave? If the system's logic isn't clear, users can't build trust or form an accurate mental model.
AI transparency & data consent — how transparent is the AI system, and do users know the full extent of data being collected — with or without their knowledge?
Hardware Shift & Ergonomics
What's happening
Headsets are getting lighter, smaller, and wearable
Watch for
Physical fatigue during extended use — are users reporting neck strain, eye strain, or discomfort before the session ends? Comfort is now a design problem, not just a hardware one.
Weight distribution & session design — how long can a user realistically wear this, and does the experience account for that limit through pacing, breaks, or load reduction?
Scale
What's happening
Mainstream adoption is no longer a question of if
Watch for
Who was included in research? — if the sample is narrow, the design will reflect that and silently exclude everyone it didn't account for.
Is accessibility built in or bolted on? — at 80M users, a post-launch patch reaches far fewer people than a default that just works.
The Sword of Damocles
Dionysius gave Damocles everything he ever wanted — the throne, the feast, the power. Then suspended a sword above him by a single thread of horsehair.
The sword was always there — Dionysius just made it visible. The throne is power. The thread is responsibility. VR's scale is the throne. The design choices we make are the thread.
What this means for us
The technology we build will have massive impact — the question is what kind, and how we advocate for the smallest features that create the greatest effect.
The Sword of Damocles reminds us that power and responsibility are inseparable. VR has been chasing the throne since 1962. Now that the scale is real, so is the thread above it.
Discussion follows
06 — Discussion

Let's talk about this

Four questions — choose one to respond to

01
Early Warning Signs
Are there signals we should be watching for before VR's negative impacts take hold?
Smartphones quickly and vastly reshaped society as they gained widespread adoption — bringing with them shortened attention spans, mental health issues, and increasing social isolation. If VR follows the same trajectory, what early warning signals should we be watching for to mitigate its negative impacts?
02
Immersion vs. Addiction
Where exactly is the line between immersive design and addictive design?
VR is being designed to feel as realistic as possible. So where exactly is the line between immersion and addictive design?
03
Designing for Everyone
What happens to VR's foundation if we design for the full spectrum of human experience?
VR is predominantly a visual and spatial medium — but human experience isn't. If we designed for users with visual impairments, motor disabilities, and cognitive differences from the ground up, how might that change the foundation of VR technology itself?
04
Remote Work & VR
Can XR deliver the experiences that physical environments already provide naturally?
The metaverse promised to reinvent remote work through VR. But what people miss about physical spaces isn't the building — it's the ambient presence, accidental conversations, and shared momentum that happen naturally. Is there a way to design XR that delivers those same experiences?
